The issue is `empty` as a YANG type doesn't really have a bool representation. It's either present or not. In the generated classes, the choice was to represent it as a bool and only allow it to be set to true. So before, when the serialisation happened then we would fall through and cause a serialisation error.

Right now, the implementation doesn't prevent someone setting an empty to `False` at set-time, so this seemed a reasonable compromise. I think we should keep this as an error in the serialisation code either way.

For background of why `empty` works like this, it's really an XML element that is:

`<leaf />`

Clearly, this thing is either there (value nothing, it's there) or not.

- .
  - Your code is affected if you use `Events.get_events(...)` and expect a list of `dict` instead of a list of `Mapping`. For example, if you use `__setitem__` (`event['key'] = value`), `update()`, or `copy()`, or if your code depends on the `str` or `repr` of the event. Use of `__getitem__` (`event['key']`), `get`, and other `Mapping` methods is unaffected. See https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/collections.html#collections-abstract-base-classes for methods supported on `Mapping` instances.

@kentsommer I haven't tested this. But this code is modeled off of a multi-gpu example from TensorFlow.

In particular, the [`cifar10_multi_gpu_train.py`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/r0.12/tensorflow/models/image/cifar10/cifar10_multi_gpu_train.py) multi-gpu code which uses the `tf.name_scope()` construct[[1](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/r0.12/tensorflow/models/image/cifar10/cifar10_multi_gpu_train.py#L167)]
```python
    for i in xrange(FLAGS.num_gpus):
      with tf.device('/gpu:%d' % i):
        with tf.name_scope('%s_%d' % (cifar10.TOWER_NAME, i)) as scope:
          # Calculate the loss for one tower of the CIFAR model. This function
          # constructs the entire CIFAR model but shares the variables across
          # all towers.
          loss = tower_loss(scope)
          ...
```

Just to be clear about ```nodes_iter``` and ```edges_iter```.  These methods do not exist in the NXv2 graph classes. But code written for both NXv1 and NXv2 should not use these methods, just as we don't use ```dict.iterkeys()``` in code written for both python2 and python3. So these methods should not be defined in RAG if we envision RAG as working for both v2 and v1.

Leaving them out will mean that anyone using RAG with NXv1 will have those methods available (inherited from the base class). Anyone using RAG with NXv2 will not have those methods available. Anyone writing code that might work for NXv1 or NXv2 should not use these methods.

Say you get a list of items from a folderish content type, with `contentValues()` or maybe a custom method. If you use that list in a Zope2 page template or a Python skin Script, it seems good that AccessControl checks if you are allowed to access an item from that list.

Ah, I started wondering what happens if I try to circumvent this patch by simply getting a list of items, and asking for a private item by index, without bothering to use `str.format`. I see that, also without this patch, checks are done.  I tried this code in a Script Python in a Plone Site:

```
print context.contentValues()[index]
return printed
```

When I execute this script as anonymous user and pass an index that gives me a public page, I can see it just fine. (Well, `<Document at public>` here.) When instead I pass an index that would give me a private page, I get an Unauthorized. When I try that as admin user, I see it fine again.

I can partially get around that by doing a slice, because `guarded_getitem` does not check them: `context.contentValues()[4:]` will give me something like `[<Document at /Plone/private>]`. But that is still only a list, so when I want to actually do something with it, like accessing the Title, I need to get the item, which will properly call `guarded_getitem` again, which will block me.

So it looks like values are checked after all, at least in this example case.

Note that `guarded_getitem` does no checks when both the container and the contained items are simple Python objects, which is why I had to test this with a `PersistentList` instead of a standard Python list.

One pattern that might be helpful is to use the context manager provided by `assertRaises`. For example
```python
with self.assertRaises(MyError) as cm:
  call()
self.assertIn('my error is the best error', cm.exception)
```

Then you can rewrite the above `try, fail, except, assert` as

```python
with self.assertRaises(TypedDatatypeInstanceConstructionError) as cm:
  SomeTypedDatatype()

self.assertIn('my_val', str(cm.exception))
self.assertIn('missing field', str(cm.exception)) # or something like it
```

A spot a potential problem here. 

1. Consider if the user adds a library `lib2` to their `run.py` file and runs once. The library will be added to `library.cfg` and will not be considered a built-in library.

2. The user removes `lib2` from their `run.py` and runs again. `vlist` will read `library.cfg` and discover `lib2` which will be added as a built-in library. This means any references to `lib2` will be ignored by VUnit instead of flagged as errors which it should since the library is not there anymore.

The possible solution would maybe be a bit out of scope of this PR. But I think that the `RiviveraProInterface` should not depend on the state of the `library.cfg` file in the form of reading it but rather always overwrite it regardless of what was there before. The built-in libraries can be discovered by running `vlist` without any `library.cfg` which will just show the built-in libraries.

Yes, I mean `Gatekeeper or Observer`.
If a client didn't receive a Reply in a timeout from a Gatekeeper/Observer, he assumes that it's malicious and re-sends it with the same reqId. The pool should be able to correctly process requests with the same reqId (it already can do this using a seqno DB) and just Reply immediately if the request was actually ordered.
If a client receives incorrect result from a malicious Observer, then it will be easily detected because of State Proof and BLS multi-signatures for write requests.

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/logging/__init__.py#L1721-L1728:

```python
    def log(self, level, msg, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Delegate a log call to the underlying logger, after adding
        contextual information from this adapter instance.
        """
        if self.isEnabledFor(level):
            msg, kwargs = self.process(msg, kwargs)
            self.logger.log(level, msg, *args, **kwargs)
```

So `process` is only called if the relevant logging level is enabled.

Will do. One question, do you have a reference for the order I should follow? You've asked me to move a few and I'm doing it blindly, but I'm a bit lost on the reasoning behind it. I'm following the [PEP 8 recommended ordering](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#imports):

```
Imports should be grouped in the following order:

1. Standard library imports.
2. Related third party imports.
3. Local application/library specific imports.
```

Which would place distutils (standard library) above coremltools (third party) instead of the other way around.
